design_philosophy.core_loop: Pilot a mecha suit through linear mission-based stages, switching between weapon loadouts to overcome environment hazards and enemy forces while managing suit capabilities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Mission success/failure system where player must complete optional side objectives to unlock the good ending; environmental destruction creates dynamic hazards
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Controlling a powerful humanoid mecha suit as a soldier fighting in a resource-driven global war; piloting feels weighty and distinct from standard platformers
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game enforces narrative consequences through branching endings tied to mission completion rather than typical lives/continues systems. Designer Satoshi Nakai mandated destructible environments as core to level design. The suit handles differently than human characters—slower but more capable—making it a statement about mecha physics

difficulty.lives_system: Mission-based checkpoint system; failure of any mission objective (including side objectives) triggers bad ending path
difficulty.continues_system: Stages can be replayed, but failure accumulates toward the bad ending; encourages perfection rather than infinite retries
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based with mid-stage checkpoints implied; player must complete mission objectives within stage to maintain good ending path
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player chooses which weapon loadout to equip before missions, affecting approach to challenges. Destructible environments allow environmental problem-solving. Side missions are optional but required for good ending, giving skilled players control over narrative outcome

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon acquisition system: player gains new weapons/accessories as missions progress (Vulcan, Laser, special equipment). Weapons can be swapped between missions
progression.unlock_mechanism: Missions unlock sequentially; completing objectives unlocks access to stronger weapons and new suit capabilities (land and minor air abilities)
replayability_hooks: Two distinct endings (bad ending if any mission fails, good ending if all objectives complete)
Multiple weapon loadouts create different tactical approaches to same stages
Destructible environments reward exploration and experimentation across replays
Hidden mechanics (e.g., soldier rescue connections to late-game boss) encourage attention to narrative details

world_building_techniques: Opening text crawl establishes geopolitical conflict (fossil fuel scarcity, Axis vs Federation war, moon territorial disputes)
Mission briefings contextualize objectives within larger war narrative
Character relationships (Jake/Crea partnership, rivalry with Major Beldark) develop through mission events
Environmental storytelling: rescued enemy soldier's identity reveals in final boss encounter; space stations and weapons of mass destruction shown through stages
Ending cinematics shift based on mission success, making narrative contingent on player performance

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters tied to narrative progression; Major Beldark in his Bildvorg suit serves as persistent antagonist across multiple encounters, escalating from introduction to final battle. Boss design emphasizes mecha-scale combat with unique movement patterns
notable_bosses: Major Beldark (Bildvorg mecha) - primary antagonist, encountered multiple times, kills Jake's ally Apollo offscreen before final confrontation
Arc Nova - stage hazard/boss that can fail if not stopped properly, affecting ending
Enemy space shuttle (stage 5) - mission objective to prevent escape; failure triggers bad ending

faction_indicators: Jake fights for Pacific States' Marine Corp (Federation forces)
Enemy Axis forces led by Major Beldark
Allied front lines referenced as strategic objective
Warship Versis serves as faction command center and emotional anchor
danger_signals: Global resource war for dwindling fossil fuels
Weapons of mass destruction present in conflict
Space stations weaponized in orbital warfare
Mission failure consequences: crew casualties, ship damage (Versis critically damaged in bad ending), death of companion Crea
environmental_storytelling: Mountain bases in Alps showing territorial control
Space environments and orbital stations demonstrating faction technological reach
Mecha wreckage throughout stages indicating previous battles
Destructible infrastructure (buildings, bridges) shows war's impact on civilian environments
Suit customization and weapon variety reflects industrial military capacity
